summary: DHH reflects on the enormous economic value created by Ruby on Rails, noting that companies worth hundreds of billions were built with his framework. He acknowledges the temptation to feel shortchanged for not capturing more of that value personally, but argues this thinking is poisonous to the open source ethos. Instead, he finds satisfaction in the thriving ecosystem of happy programmers and successful businesses. He frames contentment with 'enough' as a special kind of wealth and defines the open source spirit as deliberately capturing far less than you create.
key_points: Companies like Shopify, GitHub, Coinbase and others built billion-dollar businesses on Rails, representing enormous value DHH helped enable
The temptation to calculate what you're 'owed' from open source contributions is lethal to the open source spirit
Gratitude grievances create a haunting, never-satisfied mindset where everyone else's success diminishes your own
The real reward of open source is the growing ecosystem of programmers and businesses it enables
Having 'enough' is a special kind of wealth that frees you from value-capture envy
money_quotes: This line of thinking is lethal to the open source spirit.
The moment you go down the path of gratitude grievances, you'll see ungrateful ghosts everywhere.
Don't let the success of others diminish your satisfaction with your own efforts.
I want for very little, because I've been blessed sufficiently. That's a special kind of wealth: Enough.
To let a billion lemons go unsqueezed. To capture vanishingly less than you create.
To marvel at a vast commons of software, offered with no strings attached, to any who might wish to build.
Thou shall not lust after thy open source's users and their success.
key_insight: The open source spirit means deliberately capturing far less value than you create, finding wealth in sufficiency rather than maximizing personal extraction from communal tools.
